english: Neuter noun. No plural.\n'Heim' (home) + 'Weh' (pain/ache).
'Heim' (home) + 'Weh' (pain/ache). Literally: home-ache. Swiss mercenaries in the 17th century were the first documented sufferers. Doctors called it a medical condition. German named it perfectly.
Feminine noun. Plural: die Sehnsuchte.\n'Sehnen' (to long) + 'Sucht' (seeking).
One of the most untranslatable German words. Not just wanting something, but aching for it. 'Sehnsucht nach der Heimat' is a core concept in German Romantic literature and philosophy.
Masculine noun. Plural: die Abschiede.\n'Abschied nehmen' = to say goodbye.
From 'abscheiden' (to separate/depart). 'Abschied nehmen' (to take farewell) is a formal expression. Every German departure involves a proper Abschied. Leaving without one is considered rude.
Masculine noun. Plural: die Neuanfaenge.\n'Neu' (new) + 'Anfang' (beginning).
Germany knows Neuanfaenge: 1945, 1990, personal reinventions. 'Einen Neuanfang wagen' (to dare a new beginning). The compound captures hope and courage in one word.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Kind' (child) + '-heit' (state of being).
'Kind' (child) + '-heit' (state of being). The -heit suffix creates abstract states: the state of being a child. 'Die glückliche Kindheit' is a universal longing across cultures.
Feminine noun. No plural.\nAlso the grammatical term for past tense.
'Vergangen' (past/gone) + '-heit.' The state of being gone. Also the grammatical term for past tense in German. 'Die Vergangenheit loslassen' (to let go of the past) is easier said than done.
Feminine noun. No plural.\nNearly untranslatable. Deeper than 'safety.'
'Geborgen' (sheltered/safe) + '-heit.' Nearly untranslatable. Not just safety but emotional warmth and protection. A child in its mother's arms feels Geborgenheit. Adults search for it forever.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Verbunden' (connected) + '-heit.'
'Verbunden' (connected) + '-heit.' The state of being bound together. 'Tiefe Verbundenheit' (deep connection) describes the invisible threads between people and places.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Vertraut' (familiar/trusted) + '-heit.'
'Vertraut' (familiar/trusted) + '-heit.' The warmth of the known. When a street, a smell, a voice feels immediately recognizable. The state of trusting deeply.
Feminine noun. Plural: die Wurzeln.\nBotanical and metaphorical.
Same Germanic root as English 'root.' 'Seine Wurzeln kennen' (to know your roots). Trees need roots, people need Wurzeln. 'Entwurzelt' (uprooted) describes losing them.
Adjective. From Greek 'nostos' (return) + 'algos' (pain).\n'Ein nostalgischer Blick' (a nostalgic look).
From Greek 'nostos' (return home) + 'algos' (pain). The Greek roots mean exactly the same as Heimweh: home-pain. The pain and pleasure of remembering.
Adjective. From 'vertrauen' (to trust).\n'Eine vertraute Stimme' (a familiar voice).
From 'vertrauen' (to trust). What you trust is what feels familiar. The adjective captures both trust and recognition. 'Vertraute Umgebung' (familiar surroundings) means safety.
Adjective. 'Un-' (not) + 'vergesslich' (forgettable).\n'Ein unvergesslicher Moment.'
'Un-' (not) + 'vergesslich' (forgettable). What cannot be forgotten. The un- prefix makes it absolute: memory refuses to let go. 'Ein unvergessliches Erlebnis' is the highest praise.
Adjective. From Greek 'melancholia' (black bile).\n'Eine melancholische Stimmung.'
From Greek 'melancholia' (black bile). The ancient Greeks believed sadness came from black bile. German adopted the word for that sweet, contemplative sadness that is not despair.
Regular verb. Not separable.\n'Ich vermisse Berlin' (I miss Berlin).
'Ver-' + 'missen' (to miss). Not separable. The ver- prefix intensifies the absence. You do not just notice something missing. You feel the gap it left behind.
Strong verb: empfinden / empfand / empfunden.\nDeeper than 'fühlen': emotional finding.
'Emp-' (in) + 'finden' (to find). Finding something within yourself. Deeper than fühlen: empfinden is emotional discovery. Strong verb: empfinden / empfand / empfunden.
Regular verb. Reflexive: 'sich erinnern an + Akk.'\n'Sich an die Kindheit erinnern.'
'Er-' + 'inner' (inner). You bring something back into your inner self. Making the past internal again. Reflexive with an + Akkusativ: 'Ich erinnere mich an den Tag.'
Regular verb. Always reflexive: 'sich sehnen nach + Dat.'\n'Sich nach der Heimat sehnen.'
The verb behind Sehnsucht. Always reflexive with nach. 'Sich nach der Heimat sehnen' (to long for home). A deep, slow ache that does not go away. The most German of emotions.
Neuter noun. No plural.\n'Fern' (far) + 'Weh' (ache). The opposite of Heimweh.
'Fern' (far/distant) + 'Weh' (ache). The opposite of Heimweh. Not homesickness but 'farsickness.' Only German has a single word for the ache of wanting to be somewhere far away.
Feminine noun. No plural.\nFrom Greek 'nostos' (return) + 'algos' (pain).
Same roots as 'nostalgisch.' Originally a medical diagnosis for Swiss soldiers missing home. Now: the bittersweet pleasure of remembering what once was.
Feminine noun. Plural: die Nachbarschaften.\n'Nachbar' (neighbor) + '-schaft' (collective).
'Nachbar' (neighbor) + '-schaft' (collective). 'Gute Nachbarschaft' (good neighborliness) is a German virtue. Your Nachbarschaft defines your daily Heimat more than your country does.
Feminine noun. Plural: die Landschaften.\n'Land' (land) + '-schaft.'
'Land' (land) + '-schaft.' Caspar David Friedrich painted Germany's Romantic Landschaften. The word also means 'region' in political history. From North Sea to Alps, one word covers it all.
Feminine noun. Plural: die Atmosphären.\nBoth literal (air) and figurative (mood).
From Greek 'atmos' (vapor) + 'sphaira' (sphere). Both literal (air around Earth) and figurative (mood of a place). 'Die Atmosphäre dieses Ortes' captures what makes a place feel alive.
Feminine noun. Plural: die Umgebungen.\n'Um' (around) + 'Gebung' (surrounding).
'Um' (around) + 'Gebung' (giving/surrounding). What gives itself around you. Your physical context. 'Die nähere Umgebung' (the immediate surroundings) is what you see every day.
Neuter noun. Plural: die Andenken.\n'An' (at) + 'Denken' (thinking).
'An' (at) + 'Denken' (thinking). Thinking back at something. A physical object that carries memory. 'Ein Andenken an die Heimat' (a memento of home).
Feminine noun. Plural: die Praegungen.\n'Praegen' (to stamp/shape) + '-ung.'
'Praegen' (to stamp/shape) + '-ung.' What got stamped into you during childhood. Konrad Lorenz studied Praegung in geese: the first thing they see, they follow. Humans have Praegung too.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Einsam' (lonely) + '-keit.'
'Einsam' (lonely) + '-keit.' 'Die Einsamkeit der Fremde' (the loneliness of being abroad). Einsamkeit can be negative (loneliness) or positive (solitude). Context and choice decide.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Ent-' (away from) + 'Fremd' (foreign) + '-ung.'

'Ent-' (away from) + 'Fremd' (foreign) + '-ung.' Becoming foreign to what was once familiar. Marx used Entfremdung for workers alienated from their labor. Personally: when home no longer feels like home.
Neuter noun. Plural: die Lebensgefühle.\n'Lebens' (life's) + 'Gefühl' (feeling). Fugen-s.
Nearly untranslatable. The overall emotional quality of your existence. 'Das Berliner Lebensgefühl' (the Berlin vibe). The Fugen-s links Leben to Gefühl in this compound.
Feminine noun. Plural: die Lebensphasen.\n'Lebens' (life's) + 'Phase.' Fugen-s.
'Lebens' (life's) + 'Phase.' Kindheit, Jugend, Erwachsenenalter, Alter: each a Lebensphase. The Fugen-s connects the compound. German names every stage of life.
Adjective. 'Boden' (ground) + 'ständig' (standing).\n'Ein bodenständiger Mensch.'
'Boden' (ground) + 'ständig' (standing). Standing firmly on the ground. Not pretentious. Connected to the earth and to reality. The highest compliment in rural Germany.
Adjective. 'Ein' (in) + 'heimisch' (homely).\n'Die einheimische Bevoelkerung.'
'Ein' (in) + 'heimisch' (homely). Those who are at home here. The opposite of fremd. Being einheimisch means this is your Heim. Used as noun: 'die Einheimischen' (the locals).
Adjective. 'Heimat' (home) + 'verbunden' (connected).\n'Ein heimatverbundener Mensch.'
'Heimat' (home) + 'verbunden' (connected). Connected by invisible bonds to the place that shaped you. A compound adjective that captures loyalty to origin.
Present participle of 'praegen.'\n'Ein praegendes Erlebnis' (a formative experience).
Present participle of 'praegen.' Something that stamps itself into you. 'Die praegenden Jahre' (the formative years). What marks you permanently.
Adjective. 'Heimat' + '-los' (without).\nNot 'obdachlos' (without shelter). Deeper.
'Heimat' + '-los' (without). Not 'obdachlos' (without shelter). Heimatlos is deeper: having no place that feels like home. Stateless refugees are heimatlos. It describes the soul, not the roof.
Adjective. From 'sitzen' (to sit) + '-haft' (stuck).\n'Sesshaft werden' (to settle down).
From 'sitzen' (to sit) + '-haft' (stuck/attached). Literally: stuck sitting. When nomads stopped moving, they became sesshaft. 'Sesshaft werden' means to establish a home.
Feminine noun. No plural in common use.\n'Rueck' (back) + 'Kehr' (turn).
'Rueck' (back) + 'Kehr' (turn). You turn back toward where you came from. Every Odyssey ends with a Rückkehr. The word itself mirrors the journey: going back.
Masculine noun. Plural: die Aufbrueche.\n'Auf' (up) + 'Bruch' (break).
'Auf' (up) + 'Bruch' (break). Breaking up from where you are to go somewhere new. 'Der Aufbruch in ein neues Leben' (setting out into a new life). Breaking open, not breaking down.
Masculine noun. Plural: die Umzuege.\n'Um' (re-/around) + 'Zug' (pull/move).
'Um' (re-/around) + 'Zug' (pull/move). You pull your life around to a new location. Germans move 4.5 times in their lifetime on average. 'Umzugskartons' (moving boxes) are a German life staple.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Sinn' (meaning) + 'Suche' (search).
'Sinn' (meaning) + 'Suche' (search). The existential search for purpose. 'Auf Sinnsuche sein' (to be searching for meaning). Deeply embedded in German philosophy from Hegel to Heidegger.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Un-' + 'Gewissheit' (certainty).
'Un-' + 'Gewissheit' (certainty). Not knowing what comes next. The un- prefix negates certainty absolutely. 'Die Ungewissheit der Zukunft' is the human condition.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Vergaenglich' (transient) + '-keit.'
'Vergaenglich' (transient) + '-keit.' Everything passes. German Romantic poetry is obsessed with Vergaenglichkeit. The beauty of a flower is in its fading.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Vor' (before) + 'Freude' (joy).
'Vor' (before) + 'Freude' (joy). 'Vorfreude ist die schoenste Freude' (Anticipation is the best joy). Only German has one word for the happiness of looking forward to something.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Weh' (pain) + 'Mut' (spirit/mood).
'Weh' (pain) + 'Mut' (spirit/mood). Pain-spirit. A gentler form of sadness: not despair but a tender ache. Looking at old photos with Wehmut. Sweet sorrow.
Feminine noun. No plural.\n'Zufrieden' (content) + '-heit.'
'Zufrieden' (content/satisfied) + '-heit.' 'Zu Frieden' = toward peace. Being content means being at peace with what you have. 'Innere Zufriedenheit' (inner contentment) is the goal.
Masculine noun. No plural.\n'Zusammen' (together) + 'Halt' (hold).
'Zusammen' (together) + 'Halt' (hold). Holding together. What keeps a group unified when things get difficult. 'Familienzusammenhalt' (family cohesion) is the invisible glue.
Adjective. 'Wehmut' + '-ig.'\n'Ein wehmeutiger Blick' (a wistful glance).
'Wehmut' + '-ig.' Full of Wehmut. The adjective captures that tender ache of remembering beautiful things that are gone. Not despair, but sweet sorrow.
Past participle of 'bergen' (to shelter).\n'Sich geborgen fühlen.'
Past participle of 'bergen' (to shelter/rescue). The adjective behind Geborgenheit. What you feel in your mother's arms or your childhood home. More than safe: emotionally held.
Past participle of 'liebgewinnen.'\n'Eine liebgewonnene Gewohnheit.'

'Lieb' (dear) + 'gewonnen' (won/gained). You won something into your heart over time. Not love at first sight, but love through familiarity. 'Eine liebgewonnene Gewohnheit' is a habit close to your heart.
Adjective. 'Un-' + 'ersetzlich' (replaceable).\n'Ein unersetzlicher Mensch.'
'Un-' + 'ersetzlich' (replaceable). What cannot be substituted. Nothing else fills the same space. The un- prefix creates the absolute.
Separable, strong verb.\naufwachsen / wuchs auf / ist aufgewachsen.\n'In Berlin aufwachsen.'
'Auf' (up) + 'wachsen' (to grow). Growing upward. Separable: 'Ich wuchs in einem Dorf auf.' Strong verb: aufwachsen / wuchs auf / ist aufgewachsen.
Separable, strong verb.\naufbrechen / brach auf / ist aufgebrochen.
'Auf' (up/open) + 'brechen' (to break). Breaking open the door to something new. Separable. Strong verb: aufbrechen / brach auf / ist aufgebrochen. Not breaking down, but breaking free.
Not separable. Uses sein in perfect.\nrückkehren / kehrte zurück / ist zurückgekehrt.
'Rueck' (back) + 'kehren' (to turn). Not separable. Uses sein in perfect: 'Ich bin zurückgekehrt.' Turning back toward origin. The verb version of Rückkehr.
Separable. zurückdenken / dachte zurück / zurückgedacht.\n'An die Kindheit zurückdenken.'
'Zurück' (back) + 'denken' (to think). Separable: 'Ich denke an die alten Zeiten zurück.' Sending your thoughts backward through time. The mental version of Rückkehr.
Subordinating conjunction. Verb goes to the end.\nLinks two events: the second happens immediately after the first.

'Sobald' comes from 'so' + 'bald' (so soon). It implies the second action follows the first with no delay. In contrast, 'wenn' is general and 'als' is for single past events. 'Sobald' is the urgency connector.
All temporal connectors send the verb to the end of the subordinate clause. With nachdem, the tense shift is obligatory.
Stolz auf + Akkusativ.\n'Ich bin stolz auf meine Fortschritte.'
The preposition auf demands accusative. You direct your pride ONTO something. The most common Adj+Praep combination in German. 'Stolz auf' is the first one every learner should know.
Abhängig von + Dativ.\n'Abhängig von den Umständen.'
Von always takes dative. Hanging from something: your fate hangs from external factors. 'Unabhängig' (independent) is the opposite. The prefix un- frees you from the dependency.
Interessiert an + Dativ.\n'Interessiert an neuen Erfahrungen.'
An + Dativ when it means 'at/in.' Your interest is directed at something. 'Woran bist du interessiert?' uses the da-/wo- compound for an + Dativ.
Überzeugt von + Dativ.\n'Überzeugt von dieser Idee.'
Von + Dativ. Your conviction comes from something. The evidence convinced you. 'Davon überzeugt' uses the da-compound to reference what you believe in.
Zufrieden mit + Dativ.\n'Zufrieden mit meinem Leben.'
Mit always takes dative. Contentment goes together with its source. 'Zu Frieden' = toward peace. When you are zufrieden mit something, you are at peace with it.
Verantwortlich für + Akkusativ.\n'Verantwortlich für das Ergebnis.'
Für takes accusative. You answer for something. 'Verantwortlich' comes from 'Antwort' (answer). Being responsible means being the one who answers for the result.
Begeistert von + Dativ.\n'Begeistert von Berlin.'
Your enthusiasm comes from Berlin. Von + Dativ. 'Begeistert' literally means 'filled with spirit' (Geist). When Berlin fills you with its Geist, you are begeistert.
Enttäuscht von + Dativ.\n'Enttäuscht von der Rückkehr.'
The disappointment originates from the experience. Von + Dativ. 'Ent-täuscht' means 'de-deceived.' You were under an illusion, and reality removed it.
Neugierig auf + Akkusativ.\n'Neugierig auf die Zukunft.'
Your curiosity is directed onto something ahead. Auf + Akkusativ. 'Neugierig' literally means 'new-greedy.' Greedy for the new. A beautiful word for curiosity.
Verbunden mit + Dativ.\n'Verbunden mit der Heimat.'
Your bond goes together with the place. Mit + Dativ. Deeper than just 'connected to.' 'Tiefe Verbundenheit' (deep connection) is the noun form. The invisible threads between you and a place.
Dankbar für + Akkusativ.\n'Dankbar für die Erfahrung.'
Your gratitude is directed toward something. Für + Akkusativ. 'Dankbar' comes from 'Dank' (thanks). Same pattern as the noun: 'der Dank fuer' (gratitude for).
Gewöhnt an + Akkusativ.\n'Gewöhnt an das Berliner Wetter.'
An + Akkusativ when it means 'to' (direction). You have adapted yourself to something. 'Sich an etwas gewöhnen' (to get used to something) is the verb form.
Neidisch auf + Akkusativ.\n'Neidisch auf den Erfolg anderer.'

Your envy is directed onto someone else's achievement. Auf + Akkusativ. 'Neid' (envy) is one of the seven deadly sins. German distinguishes 'Neid' (resentful envy) from 'Eifersucht' (jealousy in love).
Vertraut mit + Dativ.\n'Vertraut mit der deutschen Kultur.'
Your familiarity goes together with the subject. Mit + Dativ. From 'vertrauen' (to trust). What you know well enough to trust. Not just knowing about, but knowing deeply.
Angewiesen auf + Akkusativ.\n'Angewiesen auf Hilfe.' Stärker als 'abhängig von.'
